title: Motor Neurone Disease
oneLiner: Progressive neurodegenerative disorder causing combined upper and lower motor neuron dysfunction in adults — leads to respiratory failure within 3–5 years.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette**  
A 62-year-old right-handed man presents with a 6-month history of progressive, painless weakness in his right hand — he struggles to turn keys and drop objects. He notices intermittent twitching in his shoulder and thigh muscles. His wife reports his speech has become slurred over the last two months, and he occasionally chokes on drinks. On examination, you find muscle wasting in the right thenar eminence and both forearms, fasciculations in deltoid and quadriceps, hyperreflexia in the lower limbs, an extensor plantar response (Babinski), and normal sensation. Cranial nerves show a hyperactive jaw jerk and reduced palatal movement. He has no pain, no sensory loss, and no sphincter disturbance.  

**Dissect the Vignette – Pattern Clues**  

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Painless, progressive weakness | Not vasculitic or radicular (where pain is typical). Pain is not a feature – if prominent, think alternative. |
| Hand weakness with “lost key” | Classic split-hand sign (thenar > hypothenar) – early LMN involvement in MND. |
| Fasciculations (twitching) | LMN irritability – denervation. Look in deltoid, quadriceps, tongue. |
| Slurred speech + choking on drinks | Bulbar involvement – both UMN (spastic dysarthria) and LMN (flaccid dysarthria) can coexist. |
| Hyperreflexia + Babinski | UMN signs – confirm upper motor neuron lesion. |
| Normal sensation | Pure motor disease – if sensory loss, it's not MND. |
| No sphincter involvement | Autonomic and sphincter sparing (key differentiator from spinal cord compression). |
| Age >60 | Most common sporadic onset. Younger onset (<40) raises suspicion of genetic form (e.g., C9orf72). |
| Progressive (not stepwise) | Unlike multiple strokes or cervical myelopathy. |

**Atypical Presentations That Might Trick You**  
1. **Progressive Bulbar Palsy (PBP)** – Starts with dysarthria and dysphagia, limb weakness appears later. Can mimic myasthenia gravis (but no fatiguability, no ptosis/diplopia).  
2. **Primary Lateral Sclerosis (PLS)** – Pure UMN variant (spasticity, hyperreflexia, no fasciculations). Slow progression, longer survival – but can evolve into classic ALS.  
3. **Flail Arm/Leg Variant** – Asymmetric, severe LMN weakness in one limb for years before spreading. Looks like monomelic amyotrophy – but later UMN signs emerge.  

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Definition**  
Motor Neurone Disease (MND) is an umbrella term for a family of progressive, degenerative conditions that selectively affect upper and lower motor neurons. Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) accounts for ~85% of cases and involves *both* UMN and LMN. The disease relentlessly spreads, leading to paralysis of voluntary muscles, and eventually respiratory failure. Cognitive impairment (frontotemporal dysfunction) occurs in up to 15% of patients.  

**Why It Matters Clinically**  
- **Frequency:** Lifetime risk ~1 in 300; incidence ~2 per 100,000. Not rare — you will see it.  
- **Stakes:** Median survival 2–4 years from diagnosis. Respiratory failure is the main cause of death. Early multidisciplinary care doubles survival and improves quality of life.  
- **Common Pitfall:** Misdiagnosing MND as **cervical myelopathy** (sensory signs, sphincter involvement) or **myasthenia gravis** (fatiguability, ocular symptoms). Missing the diagnosis delays access to riluzole, non-invasive ventilation, and palliative care. The first referral is often to orthopaedics or neurosurgery for a “carpal tunnel” or “spine problem”.  

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The Story**  
Motor neurons — both the upper (cortical Betz cells) and lower (anterior horn cells in cord/brainstem) — accumulate abnormal TDP-43 protein aggregates. This triggers excitotoxicity (excess glutamate), oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and neuroinflammation. The degeneration is axonal (dying back) — the longest axons (to limbs) die first, explaining why distal weakness precedes proximal.  

**Mechanism → Sign**  

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|-----------|---------------|
| LMN death (anterior horn) | Muscle wasting (atrophy) – especially thenar, first dorsal interosseous, tongue |
| Denervated muscle fibres | Fasciculations – spontaneous contraction of motor units (also seen on EMG as fibrillations) |
| Loss of UMN (corticospinal tract) | Spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign – reduced inhibition of spinal reflexes |
| Bulbar motor nuclei (IX, X, XII) | Dysarthria (slurred vs nasal), dysphagia (choking), weak cough, tongue wasting |
| Relative sparing of oculomotor, sacral nuclei | No ptosis, no diplopia, no sphincter disturbance – except in very advanced disease |
| Frontotemporal neuron loss | Behavioural changes, language deficits, emotional lability (pseudobulbar affect) in ~15% |
| Respiratory motor neuron loss (C3–C5 phrenic) | Diaphragm weakness → orthopnoea, nocturnal hypoventilation, morning headache, eventually type 2 respiratory failure |

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition**  
The “split-hand” (thenar > hypothenar wasting) occurs because the thenar muscles have a higher metabolic demand and are more vulnerable to TDP-43 toxicity. Bulbar onset patients have shorter survival because swallowing and breathing are compromised early. The absence of sensory findings is a direct consequence of the disease targeting only motor neurons (sensory neurones, dorsal root ganglia are spared).  

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (sporadic) – ~90%** | Unknown; likely polygenic + environmental triggers (smoking, heavy metals? limited evidence) | No family history; age 55–75; no other systemic disease |
| **Genetic (familial) – ~10%** | C9orf72 hexanucleotide repeat expansion (most common in European ancestry); SOD1 (rapid, LMN-predominant); TARDBP; FUS | Age <50, family history of ALS or frontotemporal dementia; rapid progression; C9orf72 – often with cognitive involvement |
| **Environmental (risk factors)** | Smoking (2-4x risk); military service (head trauma, physical exertion); possibly pesticides | Exposure history; younger athletic males (military athletes) |
| **Paraneoplastic (rare)** | Anti-Hu, anti-Ri associated with lymphoma, lung cancer | Subacute, with sensory involvement (not classic MND) – differentiates |
| **Viruses (extremely rare)** | Poliomyelitis (post-polio syndrome) – not true progressive MND | History of polio, then decades later slow progressive weakness |

**Most Common Cause**  
Idiopathic sporadic ALS. You will rarely find a clear trigger.  

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria**  
- **El Escorial / Awaji criteria:** Requires UMN + LMN signs in at least one body region (bulbar, cervical, thoracic, lumbosacral) with progression and absence of other causes. Awaji allows EMG evidence of LMN to substitute for clinical LMN signs.  
- **Revised: “Hiroshima” criteria** – more practical: definite ALS = UMN + LMN in 2 regions; probable = 1 region; possible = 1 region with either UMN or LMN.  

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **EMG/NCS** | Conduction block? Denervation? Reinnervation? | To confirm LMN involvement; to exclude multifocal motor neuropathy (conducting block) | Fibrillations, positive sharp waves, fasciculations; normal sensory nerve conduction (key!). Conduction block → NOT MND. |
| **MRI cervical + brain** | Cord compression, syrinx, stroke, tumour, myelitis | To rule out structural mimics | Normal MRI (or incidental non-compressive) + typical clinical = MND |
| **Bloods** | CK (mildly elevated), autoantibodies (anti-GM1, anti-MAG, AChR), thyroid, B12, HIV, syphilis | To exclude treatable mimics | CK <1000 U/L (higher in inclusion body myositis); anti-GM1 positive → multifocal motor neuropathy |
| **Genetic testing** | C9orf72, SOD1, etc. | If young onset (<40) or family history; important for prognosis, clinical trials | C9orf72 expansion → shorter survival, cognitive decline; SOD1 → rapid progression |
| **Respiratory function** | Forced vital capacity (FVC), sniff nasal inspiratory pressure (SNIP) | Baseline, prognostication, timing of NIV | FVC <50% predicted → consider NIV; SNIP less effort-dependent |

**Single Finding That Confirms / Rules Out**  
- **Confirms:** Combination of UMN signs (hyperreflexia + Babinski) + LMN signs (wasting + fasciculations) in multiple regions with normal sensory exam and EMG showing denervation but normal sensory nerves.  
- **Rules out:** Conduction block on NCS (multifocal motor neuropathy) – treatable with IVIg.  

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour — first clinic or admission)**  
- **Diagnosis communication:** When you suspect MND, refer urgently to neurology. Do **not** put the diagnosis in discharge summary as definitive without neuro input – but do initiate the pathway.  
- **Start riluzole 50 mg PO twice daily** – It prolongs survival by ~2–4 months by reducing glutamate excitotoxicity. Must be started early, ideally at diagnosis. Check LFTs monthly initially (hepatotoxicity rare).  
- **Referrals:** Speech and language therapy (swallowing assessment), respiratory physiotherapy (cough assist), dietician (nutritional screening).  

**Ward / Ongoing (24–72 hours)**  

| Issue | Intervention | Reasoning |
|-------|--------------|-----------|
| Dysphagia | PEG or RIG feeding tube placement (when weight loss >5%, or choking/aspiration). NGT can be used temporarily. | Malnutrition worsens prognosis. PEG improves survival and QoL. |
| Sialorrhoea (drooling) | Glycopyrrolate 1–2 mg PO/SC TDS (or hyoscine patch 1 mg/72h). | Anticholinergic reduces secretions; watch for confusion, urinary retention. |
| Spasticity | Baclofen 5 mg TDS, titrate to max 80 mg/day (or gabapentin, tizanidine). Physiotherapy + stretching crucial. | Reduces pain, improves function. Avoid excessive dose – worsens weakness. |
| Pseudobulbar affect (emotional lability) | Dextromethorphan/quinidine (Nuedexta) 20/10 mg daily – UK: off-label, use SSRIs (citalopram 10–20 mg) instead. | Improves social functioning; SSRI often first-line. |
| Respiratory symptoms (orthopnoea, morning headache) | Nocturnal BiPAP (non-invasive ventilation) when FVC <50% or SNIP <40 cmH₂O. | Relieves symptoms, improves survival by ~7 months. Do not delay until hypercapnic failure. |
| Pain (cramps, joint stiffness) | Quinine 200 mg nocte (for cramps), simple analgesia, physiotherapy. | Cramps are common from LMN hyperexcitability. Quinine allowed for MND cramps despite cardiac caution. |
| Constipation (opioid + anticholinergic) | Senna + lactulose, stool softeners. | Common but often overlooked. |

**Long-term**  
- **Advanced care planning:** Discuss DNACPR, ceiling of care, symptom management, preferred place of death (usually home or hospice).  
- **Respiratory care:** When NIV fails (bulbar weakness, secretions), consider tracheostomy – but only after explicit patient discussion (rarely done in UK).  
- **Multidisciplinary follow-up:** Every 2–3 months at MND clinic (neurologist, specialist nurse, OT, PT, SALT, respiratory, palliative).  
- **Investigational drugs/trials:** Tofersen (for SOD1) recently approved; many others in trial. Refer to tertiary centre if eligible.  

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**  
- **Don’t** prescribe muscle relaxants that depress respiration (e.g., benzodiazepines for spasticity – use baclofen/gabapentin instead).  
- **Don’t** give high-dose corticosteroids or IVIg unless proven alternative (they don’t help).  
- **Don’t** perform multiple unnecessary tests “just in case” – repeated MRI or nerve biopsies delay time to symptom control.  
- **Don’t** assume cognitive impairment is not present – screen with ECAS (Edinburgh Cognitive Assessment) as it affects decision-making capacity.  
- **Don’t** stop riluzole without neurology guidance – it has a small but real survival benefit.  

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | MND (ALS) | Cervical Spondylotic Myelopathy (CSM) | Multifocal Motor Neuropathy (MMN) | Myasthenia Gravis (bulbar) | Inclusion Body Myositis (IBM) |
|---------|-----------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Rare (cramps only) | Neck pain, radicular pain | None | None | Mild myalgia |
| **Exam – UMN** | ++ (hyperreflexia, Babinski) | ++ (spasticity, clonus) | – (reflexes normal or ↓) | – (normal reflexes) | – (reflexes normal/↓) |
| **Exam – LMN** | ++ (wasting, fasciculations) | ± (rare) | ++ (wasting, no UMN) | – (fatigue, no wasting) | ++ (quadriceps, finger flexors, no fasciculations) |
| **Exam – Bulbar** | Dysarthria, dysphagia, tongue wasting, jaw jerk ↑ | Dysarthria rare, no tongue wasting | – | Ptosis, diplopia, fatiguable bulbar weakness, normal tongue | Dysphagia often, but tongue normal |
| **Sensation** | Normal | ↓ posterior columns, pain ↓ | Mild parasthesiae (20%) | Normal | Normal |
| **Sphincter** | Normal | Urgency, retention | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **EMG/NCS** | Denervation, normal sensory | Normal motor/sensory; MRI shows cord compression | Conduction block (motor), normal sensory | Normal | Myopathic + neurogenic overlap (chronic denervation) |
| **Labs** | CK mild ↑ | Normal | Anti-GM1 IgM antibodies (50%) | AChR or MuSK antibodies | CK 5–10× normal, rarely autoantibodies |
| **Key differentiator** | UMN + LMN without sensory loss | MRI cord compression; sensory findings | Conduction block on NCS; responds to IVIg | Fatiguability, ocular involvement, antibodies | Weak finger flexors and quad atrophy with high CK, no UMN signs |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Respiratory failure (type 2)** | Progressive orthopnoea, morning headache, poor sleep, dyspnoea on effort; eventually acute hypercapnic coma | RR >20, use of accessory muscles, paradoxical abdominal breathing (diaphragm weakness), SpO₂ <92%, elevated pCO₂ on ABG | Urgent referral to respiratory – start NIV (BiPAP) with settings 12/4, then titrate; if unable to clear secretions, consider cough assist physiotherapy. Escalate to HDU/ICU if pH <7.25 or GCS <13. |
| **Aspiration pneumonia** | Choking after meals, recurrent chest infections, fever, purulent sputum | Focal chest signs, hypoxia, CXR consolidation, raised CRP/WCC | IV antibiotics (empiric co-amoxiclav or doxycycline), NBM, suction, consider PEG if not already placed. |
| **Malnutrition / dehydration** | Weight loss >5% over 3 months, poor intake, constipation, lethargy | Low BMI, postural hypotension, sunken eyes, poor skin turgor | Urgent dietician consult, PEG insertion; if severe, admit for NGT rehydration. |
| **Falls (motor weakness + fatigue)** | Hip fracture, head injury, fear of falling | Assess for home hazards, OT input, mobility aids, consider antispasticity medication review | X-ray, CT head if fall with loss of consciousness; bisphosphonate if fracture. |
| **Depression / anxiety** | Low mood, agitation, refusal of care, anhedonia | Screening with PHQ-9/GAD-7 | SSRIs (citalopram 10–20 mg), psychology, MND nurse support; watch for pseudobulbar affect mimicking depression. |

**Late-Presentation Patterns (the “what happens if untreated” stems)**  
- Patient presents in ED with confusion, drowsy, asterixis – gasping breathing, unable to speak or swallow – already in hypercapnic respiratory failure with an FVC <30%. **Immediate NIV and ABG, then urgent palliative discussion** — not for intubation unless previously agreed ceiling of care.  
- A patient with known MND who hasn’t had PEG and now has severe dysphagia – any attempt to swallow may cause silent aspiration → pneumonia → decompensation.  

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**  

1. **MND – “Motor, Not Sensory, Not Painful”** – The most reliable pattern: pure motor signs, no pain (except cramps), no sensory loss.  
2. **The “Spinal 5”** – Signs to remember for MND:  
   - **S**plit hand (thenar > hypothenar)  
   - **P**reserved sensation  
   - **I**nvolves bulbar  
   - **N**o sphincter disturbance  
   - **A**ll limbs affected asymmetrically  
   - **L**ower and upper signs together  
   (Actually 6 – but you can call it “SPINAL” plus the fifth is “LL” – you get the idea.)  
3. **BULBAR** – Bedside clues for bulbar onset MND:  
   - **B**reathing (orthopnoea early)  
   - **U**nable to swallow  
   - **L**isping speech (slurred)  
   - **B**risk jaw jerk  
   - **A**trophy of tongue (fasciculations)  
   - **R**eflex emotional lability  

**Clinical Pearls**  
1. **The “hand of Benediction”** – Weakness of thumb abduction (median nerve) and finger extension (radial) with preserved ulnar intrinsic? Actually no – MND spares no muscle – look for thenar and first dorsal interosseous wasting.  
2. **Jaw jerk ↑ + tongue wasting + brisk limb reflexes = MND until proven otherwise** – the combination is pathognomonic.  
3. **If a patient with “carpal tunnel” has fasciculations or loss of dexterity without sensory loss – think MND, not median nerve.**  
4. **Cough testing** – Ask patient to cough: weak, ineffective cough indicates respiratory muscle weakness. If they can’t cough strongly enough to clear secretions, plan cough assist.  
5. **Pulse oximetry at night** – Suspect nocturnal hypoventilation if SpO₂ dips <88% for >5% of sleep time.  
6. **ECAS screen** – Always do a cognitive screen (especially frontotemporal dysfunction) before making major decisions (e.g., PEG, place of care).  
7. **Don’t forget the diaphragm** – Upright FVC may be normal, but supine FVC drops significantly in diaphragmatic weakness. Measure both.  

**Common Exam/Ward Mistakes**  
- Calling MND “ALS” only – MND includes PLS, PBP, PMA (progressive muscular atrophy – pure LMN). PMA is often missed.  
- Attributing hyperreflexia to “anxiety” when it’s UMN – especially with brisk jaw jerk.  
- Not referring for NIV early because “patient doesn’t want machines”. Discuss early, because NIV prolongs life and improves sleep quality.  
- Underestimating the speed of progression – survival can be <6 months in bulbar onset.  

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team**  

| Scenario | Trigger | Action |
|----------|---------|--------|
| Acute respiratory failure | NEWS2 ≥5, SpO₂ <90% despite 15L O₂, RR >25, or pH <7.25 on ABG | Call medical registrar/critical care outreach. Start BiPAP if not already. If ceiling of care allows, consider intubation only if patient wishes (rare). Usually **palliative approach** is appropriate. |
| Stridor or complete airway obstruction | Bulbar crisis: unable to swallow secretions, stridor, panic, hypoxia | Emergency – call anaesthetist/ENT for possible emergency tracheostomy. Rare. Use suction and high-flow humidified O₂. |
| New confusion / reduced GCS | GCS <13, not due to sedation | Urgent CT head, ABG (check pCO₂), check for infection, metabolic cause. Could be hypercapnic encephalopathy – start NIV. |
| Seizure | New-onset seizure (rare in MND, but can happen due to metabolic or hypoxia) | Treat with benzodiazepine (midazolam 5 mg IM/IV), check glucose, ABG, CT head. |
| Suicidal ideation / severe depression | Patient expresses intent to harm themselves, refusal of care, or agitation | Urgent liaison psychiatry referral, ensure 1:1 observation, consider MND palliative protocol. |

**Specific Thresholds to Document**  
- **NEWS2 ≥5** – Call outreach.  
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – Concern for sepsis, hypoperfusion.  
- **GCS <13 or drop by ≥2** – Urgent assessment.  
- **FVC <50% predicted or SNIP <40 cmH₂O** – Discuss NIV.  
- **pH <7.25 with pCO₂ >6.5 kPa** – Consider referral to HDU for BiPAP titration.  
- **SpO₂ <88% on room air or >5% nocturnal desaturation** – Start NIV.  

**Key Advice:** In MND, escalation often means **palliative management** rather than aggressive ICU, unless the patient specifically wants prolonged life support. Always involve the patient (if capacitous) and

# Motor Neurone Disease

**One-liner:** Progressive neurodegenerative disorder causing combined upper and lower motor neuron dysfunction in adults — leads to respiratory failure within 3–5 years.  

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette**  
A 62-year-old right-handed man presents with a 6-month history of progressive, painless weakness in his right hand — he struggles to turn keys and drop objects. He notices intermittent twitching in his shoulder and thigh muscles. His wife reports his speech has become slurred over the last two months, and he occasionally chokes on drinks. On examination, you find muscle wasting in the right thenar eminence and both forearms, fasciculations in deltoid and quadriceps, hyperreflexia in the lower limbs, an extensor plantar response (Babinski), and normal sensation. Cranial nerves show a hyperactive jaw jerk and reduced palatal movement. He has no pain, no sensory loss, and no sphincter disturbance.  

**Dissect the Vignette – Pattern Clues**  

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Painless, progressive weakness | Not vasculitic or radicular (where pain is typical). Pain is not a feature – if prominent, think alternative. |
| Hand weakness with “lost key” | Classic split-hand sign (thenar > hypothenar) – early LMN involvement in MND. |
| Fasciculations (twitching) | LMN irritability – denervation. Look in deltoid, quadriceps, tongue. |
| Slurred speech + choking on drinks | Bulbar involvement – both UMN (spastic dysarthria) and LMN (flaccid dysarthria) can coexist. |
| Hyperreflexia + Babinski | UMN signs – confirm upper motor neuron lesion. |
| Normal sensation | Pure motor disease – if sensory loss, it's not MND. |
| No sphincter involvement | Autonomic and sphincter sparing (key differentiator from spinal cord compression). |
| Age >60 | Most common sporadic onset. Younger onset (<40) raises suspicion of genetic form (e.g., C9orf72). |
| Progressive (not stepwise) | Unlike multiple strokes or cervical myelopathy. |

**Atypical Presentations That Might Trick You**  
1. **Progressive Bulbar Palsy (PBP)** – Starts with dysarthria and dysphagia, limb weakness appears later. Can mimic myasthenia gravis (but no fatiguability, no ptosis/diplopia).  
2. **Primary Lateral Sclerosis (PLS)** – Pure UMN variant (spasticity, hyperreflexia, no fasciculations). Slow progression, longer survival – but can evolve into classic ALS.  
3. **Flail Arm/Leg Variant** – Asymmetric, severe LMN weakness in one limb for years before spreading. Looks like monomelic amyotrophy – but later UMN signs emerge.  

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Definition**  
Motor Neurone Disease (MND) is an umbrella term for a family of progressive, degenerative conditions that selectively affect upper and lower motor neurons. Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) accounts for ~85% of cases and involves *both* UMN and LMN. The disease relentlessly spreads, leading to paralysis of voluntary muscles, and eventually respiratory failure. Cognitive impairment (frontotemporal dysfunction) occurs in up to 15% of patients.  

**Why It Matters Clinically**  
- **Frequency:** Lifetime risk ~1 in 300; incidence ~2 per 100,000. Not rare — you will see it.  
- **Stakes:** Median survival 2–4 years from diagnosis. Respiratory failure is the main cause of death. Early multidisciplinary care doubles survival and improves quality of life.  
- **Common Pitfall:** Misdiagnosing MND as **cervical myelopathy** (sensory signs, sphincter involvement) or **myasthenia gravis** (fatiguability, ocular symptoms). Missing the diagnosis delays access to riluzole, non-invasive ventilation, and palliative care. The first referral is often to orthopaedics or neurosurgery for a “carpal tunnel” or “spine problem”.  

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The Story**  
Motor neurons — both the upper (cortical Betz cells) and lower (anterior horn cells in cord/brainstem) — accumulate abnormal TDP-43 protein aggregates. This triggers excitotoxicity (excess glutamate), oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and neuroinflammation. The degeneration is axonal (dying back) — the longest axons (to limbs) die first, explaining why distal weakness precedes proximal.  

**Mechanism → Sign**  

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|-----------|---------------|
| LMN death (anterior horn) | Muscle wasting (atrophy) – especially thenar, first dorsal interosseous, tongue |
| Denervated muscle fibres | Fasciculations – spontaneous contraction of motor units (also seen on EMG as fibrillations) |
| Loss of UMN (corticospinal tract) | Spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign – reduced inhibition of spinal reflexes |
| Bulbar motor nuclei (IX, X, XII) | Dysarthria (slurred vs nasal), dysphagia (choking), weak cough, tongue wasting |
| Relative sparing of oculomotor, sacral nuclei | No ptosis, no diplopia, no sphincter disturbance – except in very advanced disease |
| Frontotemporal neuron loss | Behavioural changes, language deficits, emotional lability (pseudobulbar affect) in ~15% |
| Respiratory motor neuron loss (C3–C5 phrenic) | Diaphragm weakness → orthopnoea, nocturnal hypoventilation, morning headache, eventually type 2 respiratory failure |

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition**  
The “split-hand” (thenar > hypothenar wasting) occurs because the thenar muscles have a higher metabolic demand and are more vulnerable to TDP-43 toxicity. Bulbar onset patients have shorter survival because swallowing and breathing are compromised early. The absence of sensory findings is a direct consequence of the disease targeting only motor neurons (sensory neurones, dorsal root ganglia are spared).  

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (sporadic) – ~90%** | Unknown; likely polygenic + environmental triggers (smoking, heavy metals? limited evidence) | No family history; age 55–75; no other systemic disease |
| **Genetic (familial) – ~10%** | C9orf72 hexanucleotide repeat expansion (most common in European ancestry); SOD1 (rapid, LMN-predominant); TARDBP; FUS | Age <50, family history of ALS or frontotemporal dementia; rapid progression; C9orf72 – often with cognitive involvement |
| **Environmental (risk factors)** | Smoking (2-4x risk); military service (head trauma, physical exertion); possibly pesticides | Exposure history; younger athletic males (military athletes) |
| **Paraneoplastic (rare)** | Anti-Hu, anti-Ri associated with lymphoma, lung cancer | Subacute, with sensory involvement (not classic MND) – differentiates |
| **Viruses (extremely rare)** | Poliomyelitis (post-polio syndrome) – not true progressive MND | History of polio, then decades later slow progressive weakness |

**Most Common Cause**  
Idiopathic sporadic ALS. You will rarely find a clear trigger.  

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria**  
- **El Escorial / Awaji criteria:** Requires UMN + LMN signs in at least one body region (bulbar, cervical, thoracic, lumbosacral) with progression and absence of other causes. Awaji allows EMG evidence of LMN to substitute for clinical LMN signs.  
- **Revised: “Hiroshima” criteria** – more practical: definite ALS = UMN + LMN in 2 regions; probable = 1 region; possible = 1 region with either UMN or LMN.  

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **EMG/NCS** | Conduction block? Denervation? Reinnervation? | To confirm LMN involvement; to exclude multifocal motor neuropathy (conducting block) | Fibrillations, positive sharp waves, fasciculations; normal sensory nerve conduction (key!). Conduction block → NOT MND. |
| **MRI cervical + brain** | Cord compression, syrinx, stroke, tumour, myelitis | To rule out structural mimics | Normal MRI (or incidental non-compressive) + typical clinical = MND |
| **Bloods** | CK (mildly elevated), autoantibodies (anti-GM1, anti-MAG, AChR), thyroid, B12, HIV, syphilis | To exclude treatable mimics | CK <1000 U/L (higher in inclusion body myositis); anti-GM1 positive → multifocal motor neuropathy |
| **Genetic testing** | C9orf72, SOD1, etc. | If young onset (<40) or family history; important for prognosis, clinical trials | C9orf72 expansion → shorter survival, cognitive decline; SOD1 → rapid progression |
| **Respiratory function** | Forced vital capacity (FVC), sniff nasal inspiratory pressure (SNIP) | Baseline, prognostication, timing of NIV | FVC <50% predicted → consider NIV; SNIP less effort-dependent |

**Single Finding That Confirms / Rules Out**  
- **Confirms:** Combination of UMN signs (hyperreflexia + Babinski) + LMN signs (wasting + fasciculations) in multiple regions with normal sensory exam and EMG showing denervation but normal sensory nerves.  
- **Rules out:** Conduction block on NCS (multifocal motor neuropathy) – treatable with IVIg.  

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour — first clinic or admission)**  
- **Diagnosis communication:** When you suspect MND, refer urgently to neurology. Do **not** put the diagnosis in discharge summary as definitive without neuro input – but do initiate the pathway.  
- **Start riluzole 50 mg PO twice daily** – It prolongs survival by ~2–4 months by reducing glutamate excitotoxicity. Must be started early, ideally at diagnosis. Check LFTs monthly initially (hepatotoxicity rare).  
- **Referrals:** Speech and language therapy (swallowing assessment), respiratory physiotherapy (cough assist), dietician (nutritional screening).  

**Ward / Ongoing (24–72 hours)**  

| Issue | Intervention | Reasoning |
|-------|--------------|-----------|
| Dysphagia | PEG or RIG feeding tube placement (when weight loss >5%, or choking/aspiration). NGT can be used temporarily. | Malnutrition worsens prognosis. PEG improves survival and QoL. |
| Sialorrhoea (drooling) | Glycopyrrolate 1–2 mg PO/SC TDS (or hyoscine patch 1 mg/72h). | Anticholinergic reduces secretions; watch for confusion, urinary retention. |
| Spasticity | Baclofen 5 mg TDS, titrate to max 80 mg/day (or gabapentin, tizanidine). Physiotherapy + stretching crucial. | Reduces pain, improves function. Avoid excessive dose – worsens weakness. |
| Pseudobulbar affect (emotional lability) | Dextromethorphan/quinidine (Nuedexta) 20/10 mg daily – UK: off-label, use SSRIs (citalopram 10–20 mg) instead. | Improves social functioning; SSRI often first-line. |
| Respiratory symptoms (orthopnoea, morning headache) | Nocturnal BiPAP (non-invasive ventilation) when FVC <50% or SNIP <40 cmH₂O. | Relieves symptoms, improves survival by ~7 months. Do not delay until hypercapnic failure. |
| Pain (cramps, joint stiffness) | Quinine 200 mg nocte (for cramps), simple analgesia, physiotherapy. | Cramps are common from LMN hyperexcitability. Quinine allowed for MND cramps despite cardiac caution. |
| Constipation (opioid + anticholinergic) | Senna + lactulose, stool softeners. | Common but often overlooked. |

**Long-term**  
- **Advanced care planning:** Discuss DNACPR, ceiling of care, symptom management, preferred place of death (usually home or hospice).  
- **Respiratory care:** When NIV fails (bulbar weakness, secretions), consider tracheostomy – but only after explicit patient discussion (rarely done in UK).  
- **Multidisciplinary follow-up:** Every 2–3 months at MND clinic (neurologist, specialist nurse, OT, PT, SALT, respiratory, palliative).  
- **Investigational drugs/trials:** Tofersen (for SOD1) recently approved; many others in trial. Refer to tertiary centre if eligible.  

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**  
- **Don’t** prescribe muscle relaxants that depress respiration (e.g., benzodiazepines for spasticity – use baclofen/gabapentin instead).  
- **Don’t** give high-dose corticosteroids or IVIg unless proven alternative (they don’t help).  
- **Don’t** perform multiple unnecessary tests “just in case” – repeated MRI or nerve biopsies delay time to symptom control.  
- **Don’t** assume cognitive impairment is not present – screen with ECAS (Edinburgh Cognitive Assessment) as it affects decision-making capacity.  
- **Don’t** stop riluzole without neurology guidance – it has a small but real survival benefit.  

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | MND (ALS) | Cervical Spondylotic Myelopathy (CSM) | Multifocal Motor Neuropathy (MMN) | Myasthenia Gravis (bulbar) | Inclusion Body Myositis (IBM) |
|---------|-----------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Rare (cramps only) | Neck pain, radicular pain | None | None | Mild myalgia |
| **Exam – UMN** | ++ (hyperreflexia, Babinski) | ++ (spasticity, clonus) | – (reflexes normal or ↓) | – (normal reflexes) | – (reflexes normal/↓) |
| **Exam – LMN** | ++ (wasting, fasciculations) | ± (rare) | ++ (wasting, no UMN) | – (fatigue, no wasting) | ++ (quadriceps, finger flexors, no fasciculations) |
| **Exam – Bulbar** | Dysarthria, dysphagia, tongue wasting, jaw jerk ↑ | Dysarthria rare, no tongue wasting | – | Ptosis, diplopia, fatiguable bulbar weakness, normal tongue | Dysphagia often, but tongue normal |
| **Sensation** | Normal | ↓ posterior columns, pain ↓ | Mild parasthesiae (20%) | Normal | Normal |
| **Sphincter** | Normal | Urgency, retention | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **EMG/NCS** | Denervation, normal sensory | Normal motor/sensory; MRI shows cord compression | Conduction block (motor), normal sensory | Normal | Myopathic + neurogenic overlap (chronic denervation) |
| **Labs** | CK mild ↑ | Normal | Anti-GM1 IgM antibodies (50%) | AChR or MuSK antibodies | CK 5–10× normal, rarely autoantibodies |
| **Key differentiator** | UMN + LMN without sensory loss | MRI cord compression; sensory findings | Conduction block on NCS; responds to IVIg | Fatiguability, ocular involvement, antibodies | Weak finger flexors and quad atrophy with high CK, no UMN signs |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Respiratory failure (type 2)** | Progressive orthopnoea, morning headache, poor sleep, dyspnoea on effort; eventually acute hypercapnic coma | RR >20, use of accessory muscles, paradoxical abdominal breathing (diaphragm weakness), SpO₂ <92%, elevated pCO₂ on ABG | Urgent referral to respiratory – start NIV (BiPAP) with settings 12/4, then titrate; if unable to clear secretions, consider cough assist physiotherapy. Escalate to HDU/ICU if pH <7.25 or GCS <13. |
| **Aspiration pneumonia** | Choking after meals, recurrent chest infections, fever, purulent sputum | Focal chest signs, hypoxia, CXR consolidation, raised CRP/WCC | IV antibiotics (empiric co-amoxiclav or doxycycline), NBM, suction, consider PEG if not already placed. |
| **Malnutrition / dehydration** | Weight loss >5% over 3 months, poor intake, constipation, lethargy | Low BMI, postural hypotension, sunken eyes, poor skin turgor | Urgent dietician consult, PEG insertion; if severe, admit for NGT rehydration. |
| **Falls (motor weakness + fatigue)** | Hip fracture, head injury, fear of falling | Assess for home hazards, OT input, mobility aids, consider antispasticity medication review | X-ray, CT head if fall with loss of consciousness; bisphosphonate if fracture. |
| **Depression / anxiety** | Low mood, agitation, refusal of care, anhedonia | Screening with PHQ-9/GAD-7 | SSRIs (citalopram 10–20 mg), psychology, MND nurse support; watch for pseudobulbar affect mimicking depression. |

**Late-Presentation Patterns (the “what happens if untreated” stems)**  
- Patient presents in ED with confusion, drowsy, asterixis – gasping breathing, unable to speak or swallow – already in hypercapnic respiratory failure with an FVC <30%. **Immediate NIV and ABG, then urgent palliative discussion** — not for intubation unless previously agreed ceiling of care.  
- A patient with known MND who hasn’t had PEG and now has severe dysphagia – any attempt to swallow may cause silent aspiration → pneumonia → decompensation.  

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics**  

1. **MND – “Motor, Not Sensory, Not Painful”** – The most reliable pattern: pure motor signs, no pain (except cramps), no sensory loss.  
2. **The “Spinal 5”** – Signs to remember for MND:  
   - **S**plit hand (thenar > hypothenar)  
   - **P**reserved sensation  
   - **I**nvolves bulbar  
   - **N**o sphincter disturbance  
   - **A**ll limbs affected asymmetrically  
   - **L**ower and upper signs together  
   (Actually 6 – but you can call it “SPINAL” plus the fifth is “LL” – you get the idea.)  
3. **BULBAR** – Bedside clues for bulbar onset MND:  
   - **B**reathing (orthopnoea early)  
   - **U**nable to swallow  
   - **L**isping speech (slurred)  
   - **B**risk jaw jerk  
   - **A**trophy of tongue (fasciculations)  
   - **R**eflex emotional lability  

**Clinical Pearls**  
1. **The “hand of Benediction”** – Weakness of thumb abduction (median nerve) and finger extension (radial) with preserved ulnar intrinsic? Actually no – MND spares no muscle – look for thenar and first dorsal interosseous wasting.  
2. **Jaw jerk ↑ + tongue wasting + brisk limb reflexes = MND until proven otherwise** – the combination is pathognomonic.  
3. **If a patient with “carpal tunnel” has fasciculations or loss of dexterity without sensory loss – think MND, not median nerve.**  
4. **Cough testing** – Ask patient to cough: weak, ineffective cough indicates respiratory muscle weakness. If they can’t cough strongly enough to clear secretions, plan cough assist.  
5. **Pulse oximetry at night** – Suspect nocturnal hypoventilation if SpO₂ dips <88% for >5% of sleep time.  
6. **ECAS screen** – Always do a cognitive screen (especially frontotemporal dysfunction) before making major decisions (e.g., PEG, place of care).  
7. **Don’t forget the diaphragm** – Upright FVC may be normal, but supine FVC drops significantly in diaphragmatic weakness. Measure both.  

**Common Exam/Ward Mistakes**  
- Calling MND “ALS” only – MND includes PLS, PBP, PMA (progressive muscular atrophy – pure LMN). PMA is often missed.  
- Attributing hyperreflexia to “anxiety” when it’s UMN – especially with brisk jaw jerk.  
- Not referring for NIV early because “patient doesn’t want machines”. Discuss early, because NIV prolongs life and improves sleep quality.  
- Underestimating the speed of progression – survival can be <6 months in bulbar onset.  

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team**  

| Scenario | Trigger | Action |
|----------|---------|--------|
| Acute respiratory failure | NEWS2 ≥5, SpO₂ <90% despite 15L O₂, RR >25, or pH <7.25 on ABG | Call medical registrar/critical care outreach. Start BiPAP if not already. If ceiling of care allows, consider intubation only if patient wishes (rare). Usually **palliative approach** is appropriate. |
| Stridor or complete airway obstruction | Bulbar crisis: unable to swallow secretions, stridor, panic, hypoxia | Emergency – call anaesthetist/ENT for possible emergency tracheostomy. Rare. Use suction and high-flow humidified O₂. |
| New confusion / reduced GCS | GCS <13, not due to sedation | Urgent CT head, ABG (check pCO₂), check for infection, metabolic cause. Could be hypercapnic encephalopathy – start NIV. |
| Seizure | New-onset seizure (rare in MND, but can happen due to metabolic or hypoxia) | Treat with benzodiazepine (midazolam 5 mg IM/IV), check glucose, ABG, CT head. |
| Suicidal ideation / severe depression | Patient expresses intent to harm themselves, refusal of care, or agitation | Urgent liaison psychiatry referral, ensure 1:1 observation, consider MND palliative protocol. |

**Specific Thresholds to Document**  
- **NEWS2 ≥5** – Call outreach.  
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – Concern for sepsis, hypoperfusion.  
- **GCS <13 or drop by ≥2** – Urgent assessment.  
- **FVC <50% predicted or SNIP <40 cmH₂O** – Discuss NIV.  
- **pH <7.25 with pCO₂ >6.5 kPa** – Consider referral to HDU for BiPAP titration.  
- **SpO₂ <88% on room air or >5% nocturnal desaturation** – Start NIV.  

**Key Advice:** In MND, escalation often means **palliative management** rather than aggressive ICU, unless the patient specifically wants prolonged life support. Always involve the patient (if capacitous) and
